name: SL Exam Phase 2 - Objects, Collections, and Files Question Source
description: Exam-style Python-only single-choice/true-false questions generated from Script Languages lectures 04 and 05.

questions.question: In Python, the LEGB rule means Python searches names in the order Local, Enclosing, Global, Built-in.
questions.explanation: LEGB is Local, Enclosing, Global, Built-in.

questions.question: A variable assigned inside a function is global by default.
questions.explanation: Assignment inside a function creates or changes a local variable unless global or nonlocal is declared.

questions.question: The keyword global tells Python that assignments to that name should affect the module-level name.
questions.explanation: global binds the name to the global/module namespace.

questions.question: The keyword nonlocal is used to modify a variable in an enclosing function scope.
questions.explanation: nonlocal targets an enclosing non-global scope.

questions.question: Which namespace is checked first when Python resolves a name inside a function?
questions.explanation: Python checks the local function scope first.

questions.question: Built-in names such as print and len are checked before local variables.
questions.explanation: Built-ins are checked last in LEGB.

questions.question: In object-oriented programming, object state is represented by attributes.
questions.explanation: Attributes are variables belonging to the object.

questions.question: In object-oriented programming, object behaviour is represented by methods.
questions.explanation: Methods are functions belonging to the object/class.

questions.question: A class is the blueprint used to create objects.
questions.explanation: The lecture states that each object is created from a blueprint named class.

questions.question: An object created from a class is also called an instance.
questions.explanation: An object of a given class is an instance of that class.

questions.question: Which statement best describes a method in Python?
options: A. A variable belonging to an object
B. A module imported with pip
C. A function belonging to a class/object
D. A value stored in a list
questions.explanation: A method is a function belonging to a class/object and usually operates on attributes.

questions.question: A class definition must be present before an object of that class is created.
questions.explanation: Python needs the class name to exist before instantiation.

questions.question: The special method used when a new object is created is always named __init__.
questions.explanation: __init__ initializes object attributes.

questions.question: __init__ usually sets the initial state of the object.
questions.explanation: The constructor-like method sets attribute values.

questions.question: The first argument of an instance method is a reference to the object.
questions.explanation: This parameter is normally called self.

questions.question: The name self is a Python keyword and cannot be replaced.
questions.explanation: self is a convention, not a reserved keyword, although the first instance-method parameter must receive the object.

questions.question: For an instance w of Student, w.display() is equivalent to Student.display(w).
questions.explanation: Calling through the instance automatically passes the object as the first argument.

questions.question: Which method is called when executing Student("Ann", 34)?
questions.explanation: Creating an object calls __init__.

questions.question: Methods must always be physically defined inside the class block.
questions.explanation: The lecture shows assigning an outside function to a class attribute as a method.

questions.question: Python allows adding a new attribute to an existing object after it was created.
questions.explanation: The lecture shows w.dog = "Fuks" after object creation.

questions.question: Python allows deleting an attribute from an object using del.
questions.explanation: The lecture shows del w.dog.

questions.question: Python has strict private attributes exactly like Java private fields.
questions.explanation: The lecture says there are no private attributes nor methods; underscores are conventions/name mangling.

questions.question: A single leading underscore in an attribute name is mainly a convention for internal use.
questions.explanation: Single underscore does not enforce privacy.

questions.question: A double leading underscore triggers name mangling in Python.
questions.explanation: Name mangling makes outside access harder, but not impossible.

questions.question: Which option is the usual convention for an internal-use attribute?
questions.explanation: A single leading underscore marks a name as internal by convention.

questions.question: If an attribute is stored in an object, it is an instance variable.
questions.explanation: Instance variables are stored per object.

questions.question: If an attribute is stored in a class, it is a class variable shared by instances unless shadowed.
questions.explanation: Class variables are common for all instances of the class.

questions.question: A new class can inherit attributes and methods from a base class.
questions.explanation: Inheritance defines a new class based on an existing class.

questions.question: If a subclass defines a method with the same name as the base class method, it overrides it.
questions.explanation: The subclass method replaces the inherited version for subclass instances.

questions.question: Since Python 3.0, super() can be used instead of writing the base class name directly.
questions.explanation: The lecture explicitly mentions super() as an alternative.

questions.question: Which one is shared by default between all instances of a class?
questions.explanation: Class variables are stored in the class and shared unless shadowed.

questions.question: __str__ should return a string.
questions.explanation: The lecture states __str__ should return string.

questions.question: print(obj) uses str(obj), which uses obj.__str__() for conversion.
questions.explanation: Functions such as print(), str(), and format() use __str__ for conversion.

questions.question: __repr__ is mainly intended for debugging/unambiguous representation.
questions.explanation: __repr__ is less about pretty printing and more about unambiguous/debug information.

questions.question: If __str__ is undefined, __repr__ can be called for string conversion.
questions.explanation: The lecture states that if __str__ is undefined, __repr__ is called.

questions.question: @dataclass automatically adds common “boring” methods such as __init__.
questions.explanation: dataclass is a decorator that can generate __init__, __repr__, __eq__, etc.

questions.question: In a dataclass, frozen=True means the object is immutable.
questions.explanation: The lecture lists frozen as the option that makes the object immutable.

questions.question: To create a class variable in a dataclass, the lecture says to use typing.ClassVar.
questions.explanation: ClassVar marks an attribute as a class variable.

questions.question: Which dataclass option controls whether __repr__ is generated?
questions.explanation: The dataclass parameter repr controls generation of __repr__.

questions.question: A set is an unordered collection of elements.
questions.explanation: Sets do not preserve an indexable order for exam purposes.

questions.question: Set elements are not duplicated.
questions.explanation: A set stores unique elements only.

questions.question: {} creates an empty set.
questions.explanation: {} creates an empty dictionary; use set() for an empty set.

questions.question: set([3, 4]) creates a mutable set.
questions.explanation: The lecture labels set([3,4]) as mutable.

questions.question: frozenset([7, 8]) creates an immutable set-like object.
questions.explanation: frozenset is immutable.

questions.question: Sets are indexable using numeric indexes such as s[0].
questions.explanation: Sets have no order and are not indexable.

questions.question: Sets can be sliced like lists.
questions.explanation: The lecture says sets have no slicing.

questions.question: Sets are iterable.
questions.explanation: Sets are not indexable, but can be iterated.

questions.question: For a={1,2,3} and b={3,4}, a | b is the union.
questions.explanation: | is union.

questions.question: For a={1,2,3} and b={3,4}, a & b gives the intersection {3}.
questions.explanation: & is intersection.

questions.question: For a={1,2,3} and b={3,4}, a - b gives {1, 2}.
questions.explanation: - is difference.

questions.question: remove(e) raises an exception if e does not exist in the set.
questions.explanation: The lecture explicitly says remove(e) raises an exception if e does not exist.

questions.question: discard(e) is the same as remove(e) because both raise an exception when e is absent.
questions.explanation: discard does not raise an error for a missing element; remove does.

questions.question: pop() removes and returns an arbitrary set element.
questions.explanation: Because sets are unordered, pop returns an arbitrary element.

questions.question: Which expression creates an empty set?
questions.explanation: set() creates an empty set; {} creates an empty dict.

questions.question: A dictionary stores objects as key-value pairs.
questions.explanation: Dictionaries/maps are key-value containers.

questions.question: Dictionaries are indexed by keys.
questions.explanation: Dictionary access uses keys, not numeric positions by default.

questions.question: Only hashable objects can be dictionary keys.
questions.explanation: The lecture explicitly states this requirement.

questions.question: {} creates an empty dictionary.
questions.explanation: {} is an empty dictionary, not an empty set.

questions.question: For m={"Python":3, "Java":7}, m["Python"] returns 3.
questions.explanation: The key "Python" maps to value 3.

questions.question: For m={"Python":3}, the expression "Python" in m checks whether the key exists.
questions.explanation: in on a dictionary checks keys.

questions.question: m.keys(), m.values(), and m.items() return dynamic views of the dictionary.
questions.explanation: View objects always show the current state of the dictionary.

questions.question: Iterating directly over a dictionary iterates over its keys.
questions.explanation: The shorter loop for lang in m1 iterates over keys.

questions.question: In for lang, ver in m.items(): changing ver directly modifies m[lang].
questions.explanation: The lecture warns that changing ver does not modify the dictionary.

questions.question: Which method returns key-value pairs as a dynamic view?
questions.explanation: items() returns pairs.

questions.question: Which expression removes the value stored under key "Java" from m1?
questions.explanation: del m1["Java"] removes that key-value pair.

questions.question: Binary file content is a sequence of bytes.
questions.explanation: Binary files store bytes interpreted by the application.

questions.question: Text file content is a sequence of characters divided into lines by endline characters.
questions.explanation: Text files are character data with line separators.

questions.question: By default, open() uses text mode.
questions.explanation: The lecture says default is text mode and default mode is rt.

questions.question: By default, open("log.txt") assumes read-only text mode rt.
questions.explanation: Default rt means read text.

questions.question: Using with open(...) as f helps prevent forgetting to close the file.
questions.explanation: The file is closed automatically after leaving the with block.

questions.question: A file opened inside a with block remains open after the block ends.
questions.explanation: The lecture says the file is closed outside the with block.

questions.question: f.read() reads the whole file at once.
questions.explanation: read() reads all content.

questions.question: f.readline() reads a single line.
questions.explanation: readline() returns one line at a time.

questions.question: When f.readline() reaches end of file, it returns an empty string "".
questions.explanation: The lecture states "" means there is no more data.

questions.question:
When f.readline() returns "
", it means there is no more data.
questions.explanation:
"
" means an empty line; "" means no more data.

questions.question: The most efficient way shown for reading a text file line by line is for line in f.
questions.explanation: The lecture recommends iterating over the file object.

questions.question: File mode w means append new data at the end.
questions.explanation: w means write only; a means append.

questions.question: File mode a means append new data at the end.
questions.explanation: a is append mode.

questions.question: File mode r+ means you can write and read.
questions.explanation: r+ enables reading and writing.

questions.question: File mode b means binary file mode.
questions.explanation: b is binary mode.

questions.question: f.write(s) returns the number of characters written.
questions.explanation: The lecture states write returns the number of characters written.

questions.question: f.writelines(ls) returns the number of written lines.
questions.explanation: writelines stores a list of strings and returns None.

questions.question: f.tell() returns the current position in the file.
questions.explanation: tell returns the current position.

questions.question: f.seek(5) moves to the 5th character or byte of the file.
questions.explanation: The lecture uses f.seek(5) for moving to the 5th character/byte.

questions.question: f.seek(-3, 2) is shown as moving from the end only in binary files.
questions.explanation: The lecture says this is only in binary files.

questions.question: Without arguments, fileinput.input() defaults to sys.stdin.
questions.explanation: The fileinput helper defaults to standard input when no files are passed.

questions.question: Which mode should be used to append text to the end of a file?
questions.explanation: a means append.

questions.question: Which method should you use to read one line from a text file?
questions.explanation: readline() reads a single line.
